utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective than wo_skills. The key differentiator is J3: wi_skills correctly cites the Odoo 17-specific menu path 'Accounting → Customers → Invoiceable Time', while wo_skills uses an incorrect generic path. wi_skills also explicitly names both filter criteria (Billable = YES, Invoice State = To Invoice) in J4, which wo_skills omits. utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is substantially more effective than wo_skills across all seven evaluation items. The skill-augmented run produced Odoo-specific architectural decisions: named Kanban stages with sub-status flows mapped to the agency's phases, a concrete subtask strategy keeping parent tasks on the board, the critical warning against project-level time logging, the specific 80% budget alert threshold with PM notification, the Timesheet Approval feature with explicit invoice exclusion consequence, a two-service-product configuration for role-based billing rates, and a T&M boundary condition for fixed-price work. The wo_skills run produced a well-structured and comprehensive-sounding document but lacked every key Odoo-specific detail: stages were generic phase copies, subtask strategy was absent, the project-level logging prohibition was missing, budget thresholds were vague and PM notification was not clearly stated, the approval-to-invoice link was not made explicit, no configuration approach was given for dual rates, and no T&M limitations were assessed. The agency could implement the wi_skills template directly; the wo_skills output would require significant additional Odoo research to operationalize.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both agents completed successfully and produced the required output file.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective overall. Both runs passed all four verifier checks and produced comprehensive guides covering billable setup (J1), non-billable project distinction (J2), Odoo menu navigation (J4), and best-practice guardrails (J5). However, wi_skills outperforms wo_skills on two critical items: J3 (budget alert setup) — wi_skills explicitly specifies the 80% threshold and PM notification, while wo_skills omits any threshold or recipient; and J6 (Enterprise feature awareness) — wi_skills correctly flags Timesheet Approval as edition-dependent, while wo_skills vaguely says 'if available in your version' without identifying it as an Enterprise-only feature. The skill-equipped agent produced a more operationally precise guide with field-level specificity that an Odoo-naive operations team could follow without additional documentation.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is significantly more effective than wo_skills. The key differentiators are: (1) J1 - wi_skills provides the full approval workflow including the named queue and locking consequence, while wo_skills omits the approval-locking mechanism; (2) J3 - wi_skills correctly cites the Odoo 17-specific path 'Accounting -> Customers -> Invoiceable Time', while wo_skills uses the legacy Sales-order-based path that belongs to v15/v16; (3) J4 - wi_skills names both required filter criteria (Billable=YES, To Invoice state), while wo_skills skips this pre-invoice verification step. Both runbooks handle Ben Okafor's pending entries (J2) and invoice content accuracy (J5) adequately. Neither runbook provides the specific example of an unacceptable description for J6. Overall, wi_skills produces a more accurate, Odoo 17-specific, and operationally complete runbook, while wo_skills falls back to a generic sales-order-based workflow with incorrect menu paths.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is significantly more effective than wo_skills. The wi_skills run invoked the @odoo-project-timesheet skill and produced a template with all seven evaluation criteria addressed: named Kanban stages with rationale, a concrete subtask strategy with worked examples, task-level daily logging with the prohibition on project-level entries, a specific 80% budget alert threshold tied to PM notification, Timesheet Approval linked to invoice exclusion, role-based two-rate service product configuration, and an explicit T&M fit assessment with fixed-price boundary conditions. The wo_skills run produced a competent general project management framework with good stage naming and billing awareness, but failed on three key Odoo-specific criteria: it lacked a subtask strategy (using tags instead), omitted a specific budget alert percentage threshold (only recommending manual weekly review), and made no critical assessment of T&M limitations or fixed-price boundary conditions. The skill provided the architectural opinions and best-practice guardrails that made the wi_skills output operationally actionable in Odoo without further research.
